segments.s496-g193: به + name marks the indirect object/recipient: 'to Qeydafeh' (به = to). گوی‌ای is the vocative-imperative construction: گوی (imperative 'say') + ای (vocative particle), meaning 'Say, O...'. هشیوار زن is a noun phrase in apposition to that vocative, describing the addressee ('an alert/prudent woman').
segments.s496-g194: A string of epithets joined by the coordinating و ('and'): جهاندار labels the addressee as 'world-holder' or 'ruler of the world'; بینادل is an obscure/uncertain epithet (the reading/meaning is doubtful) and is being listed as another title; رای‌زن is another epithet meaning 'counsellor' or 'one who gives counsel'. Grammatically these are coordinated nominal epithets describing the same woman addressed by the imperative.
segments.s496-g195: بدارم is first-person present/subjunctive/optative from داشتن/داشتن ('to keep/have') here meaning 'I will/let me keep' or 'I will keep'; وفای تو is the direct object ('your fidelity/loyalty') with تو as possessor; تا introduces a temporal clause 'until/while' and زنده‌ام is 'I am alive' (زنده + ـام, 1sg), so the whole segment means 'I will keep your fidelity as long as I live.'
segments.s496-g196: روان را uses را as the definite/object marker marking 'the soul' as the object; به مهر تو is a prepositional phrase 'with/by your love' (مهر = love/affection, تو = your); آگنده‌ام is a predicative past-participle + 1sg ending meaning 'I have filled' or 'I am filled' — with روان را and به مهر تو the sense is active/resultant: 'I have filled the soul with your love' (or 'the soul is filled by your love, I am so').
segments.s496-g145: ز + لشکر begins a partitive expression 'from the army'; گزین کرد is a light-verb construction where گزین (choose) plus کرد (did) yields 'chose'; پس here is an adverb 'then'; شهریار is the subject 'the king'. Word order is V (گزین کرد) followed by temporal adverb (پس) and the subject (شهریار), a common poetic inversion.
segments.s496-g146: ازان introduces the portion 'of those' (i.e., 'from those'); نامداران is a plural adjective/noun 'the renowned ones'; رومی qualifies them as 'Romans/people of Rûm'; the numeral هزار (implied in the full phrase order) follows to indicate quantity: 'a thousand of those famed Romans.'
segments.s496-g147: زره‌دار is a compound noun 'armored man(s)'; با is the preposition 'with'; گرزه 'mace' is the object of با; گاوروی here qualifies the mace (a proper or descriptive epithet), so the whole chunk means 'the armored men with the (so-called) Gavaruy mace.'
segments.s496-g148: برفتند is a past plural verb 'they advanced/went forth'; گردان is the noun 'troop/unit' and is the syntactic subject or apposition to زره‌دار in context; پرخاشجوی is an adjectival modifier 'quarrelsome/aggressive' describing that troop. Word order places the verb before the noun for poetic effect.
segments.s496-g149: همه 'all/everyone' is the quantifier; the pair گرد بر گرد is a doubled/poetic use of 'around' (گرد ... بر گرد) surrounding the locative آن بیشه 'that thicket'; مرد is the noun 'men/people' who are gathered. The phrase means 'all the men around that thicket.'
segments.s496-g150: کشیدند is the past plural 'they drew/drew up' here used with صف to mean 'drew up a line' (i.e., formed a battle line); صف is the battle-line/formation; با + سلیح نبرد 'with weapons of battle' specifies how the line was formed. The verb governs the noun phrase 'صف با سلیح نبرد.'
segments.s496-g151: سکندر is the vocative subject 'Alexander'; خروشید is a past-tense verb 'he cried/roared out'; کای is the archaic vocative 'O' addressing someone; مرد تیز is the noun phrase 'swift man/quick hero' being addressed — the adjective تیز follows the noun, as is common in older Persian poetry.
segments.s496-g152: همی is an emphatic/modal particle ('indeed/just' or intensifier); جنگ 'war/battle' is the semantic subject of رای آیدت‌گر, where رای 'plan/counsel/thought' and آیدت‌گر is a fused conditional 'آید + ت + گر' meaning 'if it comes to you' (گر = 'if'); گریز is an imperative/elliptical command 'flee' (noun used as a command here). Literally: 'If the thought/plan of war comes to you, (then) flee.'
segments.s496-g41: همی before a verb is an archaic/poetic continuative particle (marks repeated or continuous action). رنج ما is the direct object phrase 'our suffering' with the possessive pronoun ما after the noun (classical order). جوید is a present/optative-form of the verb جستن (here 'seeks/makes seek') in 3rd-person singular, so the clause reads literally 'he continually seeks our suffering'; از بهر is a two-word prepositional phrase 'because of / for the sake of' (از taking the 'because of' sense and بهر 'for').
segments.s496-g42: Theme (subject) همه گنج گیتی 'all the treasure of the world' comes before the verbal predicate. نیرزد is a classical impersonal/3rd‑person verb meaning 'is not worth' (negative sense built into the form here), and the complement takes به + noun 'به رنج' = 'worth the suffering'. The sentence therefore states that even all the world's treasure does not merit/compensate for that suffering.
segments.s496-g43: برآنم (I am resolved) is a fixed classical verbal phrase meaning 'I intend / I am determined'. It is followed by the conjunction که introducing a subordinate clause. نسازیم is the 1st-person plural present/subjunctive form of ساختن (here 'to make' used with جنگ to mean 'to wage/make war'); in classical Persian the subjunctive here expresses the speaker's resolution ('that we not make war with him'). با او is the prepositional complement 'with him'.
segments.s496-g44: نه at the start of this segment negates the following clause. بر پادشاهی is a prepositional phrase 'against/upon the kingship/kingdom' and کار تنگ is an idiomatic object meaning 'a narrow/difficult affair' (i.e. 'trouble' or 'hardship for the realm'). کنم is 1st-person singular present/subjunctive of کردن used here as 'I do / I will make' (with نه it expresses 'I will not cause'). The whole clause says 'nor will I make trouble for the kingdom.'
segments.s496-g45: یکی here functions as an indefinite/indefinite-article-like word 'one / a certain' (used idiomatically to introduce a single action). پاسخ پندمندش is the object phrase 'a prudent/admonitory answer (for him)' — پندمند describes the reply and the attached pronominal suffix -ش marks 'his/for him'. دهیم is 1st-person plural subjunctive/jussive 'let us give' (classical 'we should give' or hortative 'let us give').
segments.s496-g46: سرش 'his head' (noun + 3rd-person suffix). برفرازیم is 1st-person plural present/subjunctive of a compound verb meaning 'set/hold up high, exalt' (here transitive: 'let us raise his head'). و coordinates the next verb phrase; پندش دهیم repeats the same verb دهیم with پندش 'his counsel/admonition' as object. Both verbs share the same subject 'we'.
segments.s496-g47: اگر introduces a conditional protasis 'if'. جنگ جوید is a verbal phrase 'seeks war' (جوید is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive of جستن used here as 'seeks / desires'). پس از پند من is a temporal/prepositional phrase 'after my counsel' (here پس takes the 'after' sense together with the following از). Word order places the time phrase after the verb, which is acceptable in classical Persian.
segments.s496-g48: به بیند uses the classical particle به + verb بیند (3rd-person subjunctive/optative of دیدن) functioning like 'let him see / may he see' or a futureive 'he will see'. Again پس از پند من repeats 'after my counsel' as a time phrase. بند من ('my bond/fetter') is the noun phrase object meaning 'my fetter(s) / my constraint' (here likely figurative). The clause thus warns that, after the counsel, he will see (or be shown) the speaker's bonds — the word order is somewhat elliptical and poetic, repeating 'پس از پند من' for emphasis.
segments.s496-g169: جهاندار (the sovereign) is the subject and بگرفت is the past tense verb 'took' (he took/ seized). دستش is 'his hand' (دست + pronominal suffix -ش = 'his'), and به دست here uses به + noun to indicate 'into/into the hand' (literally 'took his hand into [his] hand'). Word order is subject–verb–object with the possessive suffix attached to دست.
segments.s496-g170: بدان گونه is a frozen locution meaning 'in that manner' (ب + دان/ demonstrative), followed by کو ... ببست which reports the manner previously mentioned: گفت here would normally be a verb 'said' introducing reported speech, but in the manuscript parsing it links to پیمان ببست 'he bound a covenant' (ببست is past 'he bound/tied'). The clause as a whole qualifies how the sovereign acted: 'in that way with which he had said he bound the covenant.'
segments.s496-g171: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (به + او collapsed), and گفت is the verb 'said' introducing direct speech. مندیش و رامش گزین is an imperative phrase: گزین is the imperative 'choose/select', and مندیش و رامش are the two nouns coordinated by و 'and' (here meaning something like gentleness/calm and tranquility). The order is indirect object + reporting verb + quoted imperative.
segments.s496-g172: من 'I' is the subject of ندارم 'I do not have' (present tense). از تو 'from you' marks the source/target of the negated possession. به دل is a locative phrase 'in (my) heart' and هیچ کین 'no grudge/enmity' is the object of ندارم (i.e. 'I have no enmity in my heart toward you'). Word order is subject — source phrase — verb — locative — object.
segments.s496-g173: چو here means 'when' (temporal) and introduces the subordinate clause. مادرت is 'your mother' (مادر + 2nd-person suffix -ت). بر تخت زرین نشست is a simple past clause 'sat upon the golden throne' (نشست = 'sat'/'took a seat'). The preposition بر takes تخت 'throne' as its object and زرین is an adjective 'golden' following the noun.
segments.s496-g174: من is the subject 'I' and اندر نهادم is a past-tense verb phrase 'I placed/put (into)'. به دست تو دست repeats دست for emphasis: به دست تو means 'into your hand' (به + noun phrase), and the final دست is an emphatic repetition ('hand' as the thing placed). The clause means 'I put myself/what I had into your hand.'
segments.s496-g175: بگفتم 'I said' is the reporting verb in past tense, followed by the complementizer که 'that' introducing the content clause. من is the subject of the following clause. دست شاه زمین uses دست figuratively as 'power/authority' (دست = control), modified by شاه زمین 'the king of the land' (شاه = king; زمین = land/realm).
segments.s496-g176: به دست تو اندر نهم is a present-future oriented phrasing where نهم is the (archaic) first-person singular present/subjunctive of نهادن 'to put/ place' (here 'I place / I would place'), so the literal reading is 'I put into your hand'. به دست = 'into the hand of', تو = 'you', اندر = 'in/within', and هم‌چنین 'thus/likewise' closes the thought. The clause as a whole expresses the speaker's promise to put authority into the addressee's hand similarly.
segments.s496-g89: This is a short descriptive clause with the numeral first (دو) followed by the past copula بودی to state weight: ‘was two’. به مثقال gives the unit of weight (’two mithqals’). هر یک (’each one’) + به سنگ (’for each stone’) marks that the measure applies to each individual stone; the verb (بودی) agrees with the implied subject (the stone).
segments.s496-g90: چو here is the simile marker 'like/as'. یک دانه نار is a figurative image ('a single grain/seed of fire' = a glowing ember); بودی is again the past copula 'was'. به رنگ literally 'in color' (here introducing the property being compared). Word order places the simile before the copula and the quality phrase.
segments.s496-g91: This clause states the condition of the emerald: زمرد (emerald) is the subject. چار صد پاره بود literally 'was four‑hundred pieces' (پاره = pieces/fragments). برو is an archaic/poetic item here and does not function as the ordinary verb 'go' — it appears as an enclitic/connector in older usage; it is irregular compared with modern colloquial verbs.
segments.s496-g92: به سبزی introduces the quality 'in (its) greenness' (به + noun gives the quality or appearance). چو is the comparative 'like/as'. قوس قزح = 'the arch of the rainbow' (قوس = arc/bow, قزح = rainbow); نابسود is an older adjective meaning something like 'not exhausted/spoiled' (here poetically 'unfading' or 'not lost'), functioning as a predicate adjective modifying the comparison.
segments.s496-g93: گشاده (’roomy, wide’) modifies شتر بار (’camel-load’). The clause uses بودی (past copula) to predicate existence/quantity: 'a roomy camel-load there was' and the trailing چهل gives the numeric amount (’forty’). Word order is descriptive adjective + noun + predicate + numeral.
segments.s496-g94: زنی بود states 'there was a woman' (زنی = 'a woman', بود = past copula). چون موج دریا به دل is a simile: 'like a wave of the sea in (her) heart' — چون introduces the comparison, موج دریا is the image, and به دل specifies where that likeness resides (in the heart).
segments.s496-g95: دگر (’another/again’) begins the clause; چار (’four’) with the following implied صد makes 'four hundred'. تای indicates 'pairs' (تای دندان = pairs of teeth/tusks), and دندان پیل 'elephant teeth' = elephant tusks. The clause lists a further set of items (another/again four hundred pairs).
segments.s496-g96: This is an exclamatory remark: چه introduces an exclamation ('what/how'). دندان درازیش = 'their long teeth' (درازیش = their length/longness). بد here is used idiomatically/intensifying in older usage and does not carry the simple modern sense 'bad'; میل میل is a repetitive measure expression meaning 'mile upon mile' or a great length (the repeated میل intensifies distance). The clause emphasizes the extraordinary length of the tusks.
segments.s496-g57: بگفتند is an older/poetic past tense form meaning “they said” (ب‑ prefix appears in literary registers). کای is a vocative particle “O” addressing سرور (lord); داد و راد are two nouns/epithets joined by و, here meaning “justice and generosity” and standing in apposition to the addressed lord.
segments.s496-g58: This clause uses ندارد (lit. ‘does not have’) impersonally to mean ‘there is not’; کسی is ‘anyone’, and چون تو مهتر is a comparative/appositive phrase ‘a chief like you’. به یاد (here ‘in memory’ / ‘remembered’) places the phrase in the domain of remembrance. Word order is literary: verb before subject and complement.
segments.s496-g59: نگویی here is a literary/archaic negative potential/impersonal construction meaning roughly “one would not say” (it is not the conversational noun listed in the glosses, so no exact sense match). مگر introduces an exception (‘unless’ / ‘except’), آنک is the relative ‘that/who’, بهتر is the comparative ‘better’, and بود is past tense ‘was’. The clause reads ‘you would not say, unless that one was better’.
segments.s496-g60: خنک modifies شهرکش (adjective before noun) and is a poetic epithet here (literally ‘cool/refreshing’ but functioning as a descriptive epithet of the town‑ruler); چون تو = ‘like you’; مهتر = predicate noun ‘chief’; بود = past ‘was’. The clause states that the (supposed) better person was a town‑ruler who, like you, was a chief.
segments.s496-g61: اگر marks a conditional ‘if’. دوست is a predicate noun ‘friend’ and گردد (from گشت/گردد) is subjunctive/potential ‘becomes’ (here third‑person). ترا is the older/object form of تو written as ت + را (ت = 2nd‑person element, را = object/definite marker) and together mean ‘to you’ / ‘your friend’. پادشا is the subject ‘the king’ placed after the verb in classical word order.
segments.s496-g62: چه خواهد is an interrogative + verb ‘what will/would he want’, with the subject omitted (understood from the previous clause as the king). جزین = جز + این ‘except these’, and مردم پارسا ‘the pious people’ is the phrase being excluded; together they mean ‘what would he want except these pious people?’ The clause is a rhetorical question; word order (verb before object) is literary.
segments.s496-g63: نه negates the clause. آسیب آید is ‘harm comes’ (آید = comes/will come). بدین is the compound prepositional phrase (به + این) = ‘to/for this’ (tokenization in the input splits it into ب + دین; ب = preposition ‘to/into’ and دین is an artifact here), and گنج تو = ‘your treasure’ (possessive). The whole clause says ‘no harm comes to this treasure of yours.’
segments.s496-g64: نیرزد is a negative evaluative verb meaning ‘is not worth’ (takes the thing valued as subject or post‑verbal), همه گنجها ‘all the treasures’ is the subject, and رنج تو ‘your toil/suffering’ is the measure/thing compared. The line reads ‘all the treasures are not worth your toil’ (i.e. your trouble is worth far more than every treasure).
segments.s496-g185: بفرمود is an honorific/formal past verb meaning 'he said/commanded' (here: 'he ordered'). The construction بفرمود تا + clause uses تا as a subordinator meaning 'that / so that / in order that' and introduces the content of the order. خوان is the direct object 'banquet/feast', and بیاراستند is the third-person plural past form 'they arranged/laid out' so the whole segment reads 'he ordered that they set the banquet.'
segments.s496-g186: نوازنده is a noun 'musician/performer' and here names those who accompany the feast. رود in this poetic context names a stringed instrument (not a river), so نوازنده رود = 'players on the rud/lute'. و is the coordinating 'and', and می‌خواستند is the imperfect/past habitual 'they wished/desired' (narrative past plural), continuing the description after the preceding order.
segments.s496-g187: Again بفرمود = 'he ordered/commanded'. تا introduces the subordinate clause of the order ('that ...'). خلعت is 'robe of honour/robe' as the direct object, and خسروی is an adjective/attributive meaning 'royal' (i.e. 'royal robe'). The phrase = 'he ordered that royal robes ...'.
segments.s496-g188: ز (poetic 'from') begins a list of sources: رومی 'of Rum/Byzantium' and چینی 'of China'. The repeated use of ز/از ('from') is a rhythmic/poetic coordination; here از has the same basic meaning 'from'. پهلوی refers to the Pahlavi/Pahlav cultural region (here used as the third origin of the robes).
segments.s496-g189: ببخشید here is the past of بخشیدن used in the sense 'he bestowed/gave'. یارانش را is the object phrase 'his companions' with the possessive -ش and the definite-object marker را. سیم و زر = 'silver and gold' are the items given. Word order is Verb + Object (common in Ferdowsi's narrative), i.e. 'he gave his companions silver and gold.'
segments.s496-g190: کرا is an archaic relative/interrogative form meaning 'who/whom' or 'whichever (one)'; here it functions like 'whomever/whichever of them'. در خور آمد is a fixed verbal/adjectival phrase meaning 'was fitting/suited' (در + خور + آمد), where آمد is the past 'came' used idiomatically. کلاه و کمر are the items that followed when it fitted them: 'a hat and a girdle.' So: 'to whom it suited, a hat and girdle (were given).'
segments.s496-g191: به + name + فرمود is the common construction 'he said to X'. طینوش here is a proper name (addressee). کایدر is an archaic/interrogative or vocative particle here (dialectal 'Kaydar' / 'O where/what') addressing or questioning, and مه‌ایست is a poetic verblike clause 'it is the moon' (مه = moon; ایست = 'is'), so the inner quoted speech begins with an identification: '(O Kaydar) it is the moon.'
segments.s496-g192: که here functions as a causal/explanatory connector 'for/because' introducing the reason: این بیشه دورست = 'this grove is distant' (این = 'this', بیشه = 'grove', دورست = 'is far' — adjective/predicate after the noun). راه تو نیست literally 'the road/path is not yours' (راه = 'path/way', تو = 'you', نیست = 'is not'), i.e. 'this route is not for you.'
segments.s496-g81: Word order is typical Persian: numeric determiner 'یکی' (one) precedes the noun 'تخت' (throne). 'بودش' is past tense of 'بودن' with the enclitic -ش (= 'it/his'), so literally 'a throne it-was'. The prepositional phrase 'به هفتاد لخت' follows the verb and gives a measure/qualification ('with seventy l akht/folds'); in poetic diction 'به' marks the measure.
segments.s496-g82: 'ببستی' is a past-form of 'بستن' (here read as 'closed'/'shut') and appears without an expressed subject (typical in epic poetry). The following noun phrase 'گشاینده نیک‌بخت' is an epithet in apposition — 'گشاینده' is the active participle 'opener' and 'نیک‌بخت' is an epithet 'fortunate/fortunate one', so the line addresses or describes the agent as the 'fortunate opener'.
segments.s496-g83: This clause uses a verbal/adjectival past participle 'بافته' (woven/fashioned) predicatively. The phrase 'به پیکر یک اندر دگر' literally 'into the shape/figure of one into another' uses 'به پیکر' ('to the form/shape of') + 'اندر' (archaic preposition 'in/into') + 'دگر' ('another'), so the whole means something was transformed or woven from one form into another.
segments.s496-g84: 'به چاره' here means 'by artifice/with an expedient' (چاره = a devised means). 'سر' = 'head' and 'شوشها' is an archaic/poetic plural referring to Shush/Susa (i.e., Susa-related work). 'تافته' is the past participle of 'تافتن' meaning 'woven/embroidered/fashioned'. The clause states that (it) was fashioned for the head in Susa-like workmanship (typical compact noun order in poetry: manner + head + place/adjective + verb-participle).
segments.s496-g85: This simile uses 'چون' (like/as). 'سر پایها' is a genitival/compound expression 'the head(s) of the feet' (پایها = plural 'feet'). Word order is noun + modifier (چون + simile noun): 'سر پایها چون سر اژدها' = 'the heads of the feet like the head of a dragon.'
segments.s496-g86: Here the negative-past verb 'ندانست' (did not know) precedes the subject 'کس' (no one/anyone) for emphatic word order. 'گوهرش' is the possessed noun 'its jewel' and 'را' marks it as the definite object; 'بها' is the nominal predicate meaning 'price/value'. So the clause reads literally 'No one knew the jewel's price/value.'
segments.s496-g87: 'ازو' ('from it' / 'of it') introduces a genitive/partitive description; the verb 'بود' is omitted (common in poetry), so the clause has verbless nominal predication: 'ازو چارصد گوهر شاهوار' = 'from it (came) four hundred regal gems.' 'شاهوار' is an adjective 'regal/lofty' modifying 'گوهر' (gems).
segments.s496-g88: 'همان' ('that very' / 'the same') modifies 'سرخ یاقوت' ('red ruby'). The short clause-final sequence 'بد زین شمار' is compact poetic phrasing; 'بد' is here an attributive/adverbial particle used in older/poetic Persian (the exact nuance is idiomatic), 'زین' = 'of/from this' and 'شمار' = 'number/count'. Grammatically, the phrase places the ruby in relation to the previously stated count ('among/with/in this number').
segments.s496-g177: همان روز (that same day) is a temporal phrase with the demonstrative همان before روز. پیمان من puts the noun (پیمان, 'pact') before the possessor pronoun من ('my'), a common Persian order. شد تمام is a past-tense predicate: شد (past of شدن, 'became/was') + تمام ('finished/complete'), so the clause means 'my pact became complete.'
segments.s496-g178: نه خوب آید is a negative impersonal expression: نه (not) + خوب (good/well) + آید (comes) meaning 'it does not sound/fit well.' از شاه گفتار خام is a source phrase 'from the king (شاه) a raw/immature speech (گفتار خام)', with از marking origin and خام qualifying گفتار. The verb آید governs the subordinate 'from...' phrase.
segments.s496-g179: سکندر منم is the verbless identification 'I am Alexander' where منم = 'I am' (colloquial/poetic copula). وان زمان = 'at that time' (وان is an archaic/formal variant of آن). من بدم here indicates the speaker's guilt/being at fault ('I was to blame' / 'I was bad') with بدم used predicatively.
segments.s496-g180: به خوبی is an adverbial phrase 'well' (به + خوبی forming an adverbial use), بسی is an intensifier 'very/many', داستانها is the plural object 'stories/tales', and زدم is the past of زدن used here in the sense 'to perform/recite/tell' (verb at clause end). The whole means 'I told many tales very well.'
segments.s496-g181: همان روز again 'that same day.' قیدافه is a proper name (subject) and آگاه بود is a past-state predicate: آگاه 'aware' + بود (past copula) = 'was aware.' Word order is subject + predicate as usual in Persian narrative.
segments.s496-g182: که introduces the subordinate clause 'that...'. اندر = 'in/within'; کفت (کف) = 'palm (of the hand)'; پنجه = 'claw/hand/grip' or here 'hand/palm' as part of the locative phrase; شاه is the possessor 'the king'; بود is the past copula 'was'. The phrase literally reads 'that it was in the palm of the king's hand' (poetic ordering places modifiers and possessor in a compact sequence).
segments.s496-g183: پرستنده را گفت قیصر is a slightly inverted poetic order: پرستنده را (the worshipper, with را marking the addressee/definite object) + گفت (said/told) + قیصر (Caesar, the speaker). که begins the content clause and تخت is the head noun 'throne' of that clause. In narrative poetry the subject (قیصر) can follow the verb for meter.
segments.s496-g184: بیارای here is an imperative/jussive-type verb meaning 'arrange/adorn/set up' (poetic form). زیر is the preposition 'under/beneath', گلفشان ('flower-scattering' / 'flowering') qualifies درخت ('tree'), so the full phrase means 'set up (the) throne beneath the flowering tree.' Note the adjective/participial qualifier is placed before or after the noun by poets for meter.
segments.s496-g121: سکندر is the subject. به اسپ is a prepositional phrase 'onto/into the horse' (به = towards/into) that marks where the action is directed. اندر آورد is a past-tense verbal compound (اندر + آورد) meaning 'placed' or 'set', and پای is the direct object 'foot' placed after the verb (classical Persian allows the object after the verb here). The whole segment means 'Alexander placed his foot on/into the horse (i.e. mounted)'.
segments.s496-g122: به دستوری is an adverbial phrase meaning 'by order' where به takes the sense 'by'/'-ly' and دستوری = 'by command'. بازگشتن is the infinitive/verb 'to return' used here with implied subject (he); به جای marks the destination 'to his place' (به = 'to', جای = 'place'). The sequence places the circumstantial phrase before the verb, a normal classical order.
segments.s496-g123: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. طینوش here is a proper name (the agent). جنگی qualifies سپه as 'war-/military', so جنگی سپه = 'war-host/army'. برنشاند is a past 3rd-person verb 'he marshaled/arrayed/placed (in order)'. The verb follows its object (سپه) in this classical ordering.
segments.s496-g124: از ... به ... is a pair of prepositions showing movement 'from ... to ...' (از = from, به = to). ایوان names the point of departure and به درگاه قیدافه the destination ('to the court/threshold of Qeydafeh'). راند is the past verb 'drove' whose subject is carried over from the previous clause (he/they drove [them]).
segments.s496-g125: به قیدافه is an indirect-object phrase 'to Qeydafeh' (به marks the addressee). گفتند is the reported-speech verb 'they said' (the provided sense list did not include this verbal sense, see note). پدرود باش is the quoted imperative 'be farewell' or 'farewell' with باش as the imperative 'be'. The clause introduces direct speech: 'They said to Qeydafeh: "پدرود باش".'
segments.s496-g126: به جان تازه is an oath-like prepositional phrase 'by the fresh life' (به here functioning like 'by' — hence the 'by/-ly' sense). چرخ را پود is a metaphorical predication with را marking the definite object 'the heaven/sky' and پود 'weft' as the noun predicated of it; the construction (= 'be the weft of the sky') ends with باش, the imperative 'be'. The phrase is poetic inversion: the oath phrase precedes the predicative clause.
segments.s496-g127: برین گونه is a fixed classical adverbial phrase meaning 'in this manner' or 'thus' (بر + این (ین) + گونه). منزل به منزل is a repetitive adverbial 'stage by stage / camp by camp' (منزل used as 'stage' here) and سپاه is the noun 'army' associated with that movement. The overall segment is an adverbial fronting that describes how the army moved.
segments.s496-g128: همی is a classical particle often marking continued or habitual action; here it accompanies the past-tense verb راند 'drove/marched' (he/they kept driving/marching). تا introduces the extent ('until') and پیش آن رزمگاه 'before that battlefield' gives the destination/limit. The verb agrees with the army implied earlier.
segments.s496-g129: که introduces a relative clause here ('that/which'); لشکرگه is a compound noun meaning 'army camp'; نامور is an adjective postposed (typical in older Persian/poetic word order) meaning 'renowned'; شاه is the noun 'king'; بود is the past copula 'was', completing the clause 'which was the king's famed camp.'
segments.s496-g130: سکندر is the subject; the following که again introduces a relative clause 'who/which'; با + بخت is a prepositional phrase 'with fortune' (بخت = luck/fortune); همراه here functions adverbially/'together with' indicating accompaniment; بود is the past copula 'was' — together: 'Alexander, who had fortune with him.'
segments.s496-g131: سکندر is the subject; بران is a locative/old-fashioned adverbial 'there/on that spot'; بیشه 'grove' is the place; بنهاد is the past tense verb 'placed/put down' (بنهاد = بنهادن, archaic past); رخت here means 'baggage' or 'camping gear' — 'Alexander there set down his baggage.'
segments.s496-g132: که introduces an explanatory clause 'for/that'; آب روان is a collocation meaning 'flowing water' (روان = flowing); بود is the copula 'was' (past); و links the two predicates; جای درخت literally 'place(s) for trees' (جای = place, درخت = tree) describing the grove's suitability for trees/shade.
segments.s496-g133: به introduces the indirect object 'to Tinush' (طینوش is a proper name here); گفت is the verb 'said' (poetic past of گفتن) followed by the quoted imperative; ایدر (اینجا/ایدر) = 'here'; آرام گیر is an imperatival phrase where آرام 'calm/at rest' and گیر is imperative 'take/hold' (classical usage), so 'take rest here' or 'rest here.'
segments.s496-g134: چو = 'when'; آسوده = 'at ease/relieved'; گردی here is a verbal form meaning 'you become' (poetic/archaic subjunctive or jussive of گشتن) — note this token's provided sense list didn't include that meaning, so it's marked unknown; می‌و جام = 'wine (mey) and cup' (می‌و is an older/poetic form for wine/fruit, جام = cup); گیر is again an imperative 'take/receive' (i.e. 'take wine and a cup').
segments.s496-g135: شوم is an optative/jussive form 'may I be' or a formulaic oath ('may I be ...') expressing strong determination; هرچ 'whatever/whatever I have'; گفتم is the past tense 'I said' (classical past); به جای آورم is an idiom 'to bring into place' = 'to accomplish/fulfill' — آورم is the present-subjunctive/optative 'I may bring/about', so the clause expresses resolution to carry out what was said.
segments.s496-g136: ز هر گونه = 'of/from every kind' (ز = from/of in poetic register; هر گونه = every kind); پاکیزه modifies رای 'pure/clean counsel'; رای here means 'plan, counsel or opinion' (رای = counsel/plan in classical register); آورم again is 'I will bring forth/deliver' (first-person optative/subjunctive), so the clause says 'I will produce pure counsel of every kind.'
segments.s496-g33: ازان پس is an adverbial phrase meaning 'after that' (ازان = 'from that' + پس = 'after'). گرامی is an adjective placed before the noun (classical/poetic order) modifying دو فرزند. را is the definite-object marker attached to the entire noun phrase, anticipating a transitive verb (i.e. this NP is the object of 'بیاورد').
segments.s496-g34: بیاورد is a past/factual narrative verb 'brought' (classical form used in epic narration). The following nouns خویشان و پیوند را are its objects; و simply links the two objects. را after پیوند again marks the (definite) object phrase. Note the verb precedes its objects here, a permissible poetic ordering.
segments.s496-g35: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' placed before the verb; گفت is the simple past 'he said' (here the annotator's senses did not include 'say', so the appropriate sense index is not available). کاندر is an archaic fused preposition (که + اندر) meaning 'that in / wherein', introducing the locative phrase سرای سپنج (a house or place name).
segments.s496-g36: سزد‌گر ... نباشیم is an archaic conditional/hortative construction: سزدگر = 'if it is fitting' / 'it would be fitting', followed by the negative subjunctive/optative نباشیم 'that we not be' (1st person plural). چندین here quantifies the predicate ('many' / 'several'), and به رنج is a prepositional phrase 'in/into hardship' that completes the idea 'many in hardship'.
segments.s496-g37: نباید is an impersonal modal 'it should not / one should not' introducing the clause. کزین = که + از + این (fused) 'from this / by this', and گردش روزگار is a genitive noun phrase 'the turning of fate' (گردش = turn/cycle, روزگار = fate/times). Together they set the causal or circumstantial ground for the following clause.
segments.s496-g38: مرا is the experiencer/indirect object 'to me' (here the annotator's token is mismatched, so the correct sense isn't listed). را after it marks definiteness/emphasis for the following noun. بهره کین آید is the core: بهره 'a share' + کین 'of hatred/vengeance' + آید 'comes' (here used in the sense 'may come / comes to'). و کارزار appends 'and battle/struggle' as another thing that might come to the speaker.
segments.s496-g39: سکندر is the subject 'Alexander'. نخواهد شد is a future/irrealis construction here meaning 'will not become / will not be' (نخواهد as auxiliary + شد 'become'). از گنج سیر is a prepositional/complement phrase 'satiated from/with treasure' (از indicates source or means, سیر = 'sated/satiety').
segments.s496-g40: وگر introduces a contrary/hypothetical clause 'and if / but if'. آسمان is the subject 'the sky/heaven(s)'; اندر is the locative 'into / within' (here part of the phrasal idea 'into under'), and آرد is the classical/poetic form of آورد 'bring' (3rd person subjunctive/optative sense 'should bring' / 'brings'). به زیر is a directional phrase 'to/beneath' completing the image 'bring the sky down beneath'.
segments.s496-g105: 'ز' (z) marks material or origin here: 'of/from brocade and velvet'. The noun phrase gives the number after the material ('دیبای خز چارصد تخته') and ends with 'نیز' ('also'), so the whole segment reads 'also four hundred boards of brocade and velvet'.
segments.s496-g106: 'همان تختها کرده' = '(he) made those same thrones'; 'کرده' is a past participle/verb meaning 'made' placed after the object (subject is omitted). The prepositional phrase 'از چوب شیز' ('from shiz wood') follows to specify material.
segments.s496-g107: 'دگر' (= 'another') introduces a new set: 'دگر چهار صد تخته' = 'another four hundred boards'. 'از عود‌تر' names the material (agarwood / oud); here the construction is 'another + number + noun + of + material'.
segments.s496-g108: The relative clause introduced by 'که' ('which/that') describes the boards: 'مهر اندرو گیرد' literally 'take a مهر upon them' and 'و رنگ زر' 'and the color of gold'. 'گیرد' is classical-present-used-as-narrative meaning 'takes/receives' (i.e., are stamped/embossed and gilded). 'اندرو' = 'in/on them'.
segments.s496-g109: 'صد اسپ' is 'one hundred horses/steeds' (note archaic form 'اسپ'). Adjectives follow the noun: 'گرانمایه آراسته' = 'precious/valuable, adorned', giving 'a hundred valuable, adorned steeds'.
segments.s496-g110: 'ز میدان' = 'from the field/arena' (ز = from). 'ببردند' is the past tense 3rd-person plural 'they carried/took away'. 'با خواسته' here idiomatically means 'at (his) request / at his bidding' (با + noun 'خواستـه' = 'by/with request').
segments.s496-g111: 'همان تیغ هندی و رومی هزار' = 'those same swords, Indian and Roman, (a) thousand'. 'همان' (= 'the same') points back to previously mentioned swords; adjectives 'هندی و رومی' follow the noun. The numeral 'هزار' here quantifies 'تیغ'.
segments.s496-g112: 'بفرمود' is the honorific past verb 'he ordered/commanded'. 'با جوشن کارزار' = 'with battle-cuirasses'/'together with armor for battle' where 'با' = 'with', 'جوشن' = 'armor/cuirass', and 'کارزار' specifies 'for battle'.
segments.s496-g137: سکندر (proper name) is the subject and بیامد is a past-tense verb meaning 'came'. به + noun (به پرده سرای) marks direction/entry ('into/to the curtain-hall'); پرده سرای is a compound locative ('the curtained chamber' or 'court behind the curtains'). Word order is Subject + Verb + prepositional/directional phrase.
segments.s496-g138: سپاهش is سپاه + enclitic ش (possession: 'his army'); the verb برفتند is past 3rd-person plural 'they rose/went'. The phrase یک سر ز جای is idiomatic: یک‌سر 'one-and-all / each one' + ز جای 'from (their) place', here literally 'each one rose from his place' (سر used synecdochically for person/head).
segments.s496-g139: ز شادی is a causal/ablative phrase 'from/with joy.' خروشیدن here is a verbal-noun/infinitive 'to roar/cry out' used as the object or complement of آراستند; آراستند is past 3rd-person plural (آراستن) literally 'they adorned' but here functions idiomatically as 'they broke into' or 'they decked themselves with' (i.e., they put on loud cries). The order is adverbial/cause + verbal-noun + main verb.
segments.s496-g140: کلاه کیانی is a noun phrase with an attributive adjective/noun (کیانی = Kayanian/royal) modifying کلاه ('the Kayanian hats/crowns'). بپیراستند is past 3rd-person plural with the prefix بـ (poetic/old-fashioned) of پیراستن/آراستن 'they adorned' — object implied (they adorned/placed the Kayanian hats on themselves or on one another).
segments.s496-g141: که begins a relative/explanatory clause here ('for/which'). نومید and بد are adjectives preceding the head noun لشکر (a common Persian order: modifiers before noun): 'numeed bad + army' = 'the hopeless, ill/ill-omened army.' نامجوی is a compound adjective 'name-seeking' (fame-seeking) modifying لشکر. The whole phrase functions as the topic/subject of the following clause.
segments.s496-g142: که introduces the subordinate clause 'that/which.' دانست is past 3rd-person singular 'he/it knew' and governs a following that-clause. The clause structure is دانست + [که] + subject/compound + بینند (here 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive 'they see' used reportedly) + روی 'face' as the object. کش is part of a compound with باز (uncertain/archaic compound sense here); between دانست and بینند the speaker reports what was known: '...which knew that they would behold the face.'
segments.s496-g143: سپه (army/host) is the noun; با زبانها is a comitative/instrumental phrase 'with tongues/voices' and پر از آفرین is a adjectival phrase 'full of acclamations/praise' (پر از + noun = 'full of ...'). The sequence describes manner: 'the army, with voices full of praise...'.
segments.s496-g144: یکایک ('one by one' / 'each and every') is an adverb modifying the verb نهادند (past 3rd-pl of نهادن 'to place/put'). سر بر زمین is a prepositional phrase 'head upon the ground' (سر = head/person, بر = on/upon, زمین = ground). The whole clause means they prostrated/laid their heads on the ground one by one.
segments.s496-g161: This is a short reporting clause with canonical subject-first order: the speaker's name (سکندر) comes first, then the indirect object/recipient (بدو, 'to him'), then the verb گفت ('said'). The vocative کای شهریار ('O king') follows as the direct address; کای is an archaic vocative particle.
segments.s496-g162: This is the quoted question. چرا introduces the interrogative 'why'. سست گشتی is a past/resultative verbal phrase 'you became weak/feeble' (گشتی = second-person singular past/result of گشتن). بدین مایه کار here is the prepositional phrase 'in/with this sort of matter' — the printed segmentation splits بدین into ب + دین, but here بدین functions as the classical demonstrative 'with/in this', followed by مایه کار 'this kind of affair'.
segments.s496-g163: This is an assurance clause introduced by the poetic preposition ز + من 'from me / as for me'. ایمنی is 'safety/security' and بیم 'fear' are the two contrasted nouns. در دل مدار functions as an imperative injunction 'do not set/keep (them) in your heart' — مدار here is used in the sense 'do not place/hold' (an archaic/poetic imperative), with در دل = 'in the heart'.
segments.s496-g164: This clause continues the assurance: نیازارد is a negative/future-oriented verb meaning 'will not harm' or 'shall not molest'. The prepositional از من here indicates the beneficiary/target relation ('against me' / 'to me' depending on context). کسی, in combination with the negative force of the verb, is best read as 'no one'. زان تبار 'of that lineage' specifies whose members are meant.
segments.s496-g165: نگردم is a first‑person singular negative verbal form ('I will not turn/change/abandon') derived from گشتن; here it means 'I will not depart'. ز پیمان 'from the covenant' gives the object/source. قیدافه is a proper name appended (an epithet or personal name), and من is the first‑person pronoun emphasising the speaker 'I'.
segments.s496-g166: This is a general moral comment: نه نیکو بود is a classical idiom 'it is not good' (بود as the copula, here past-form used in a generalizing statement). شاه 'the king' is the subject and پیمان‌شکن is a compound predicate/adjective 'oath‑breaker' describing what would be unbecoming.
segments.s496-g167: پیاده شد is a light verbal periphrasis meaning 'he dismounted / became on foot' (شد with a predicative/adverbial پیاده). از indicates motion away 'from', and باره طینوش is the place/source 'from the back/side of Tinūsh' — طینوش is a proper name (likely a horse or mount). زود is an adverb 'quickly/soon'. Note that باره here is a lexical item whose precise sense is context-dependent (the provided sense list does not include 'back of a mount').
segments.s496-g168: This is a coordinated clause: زمین را (object marked by را) 'the ground' + ببوسید 'he kissed' (past tense), و 'and' links the next verb, زاری نمود 'he made lamentation/wailed' where نمود is the past of 'نمودن' used as a light verb 'to perform/show', so 'زاری نمود' = 'he bewailed'.
segments.s496-g1: همی is an adverb of continuous or ongoing action ('kept/continually'); چاره جست is a simple past verb phrase (جست from جستن) meaning 'sought a remedy' with the verb following its object in terse, poetic word order; آن شب دیریاز is a temporal phrase ('that long night') placed after the verb for emphasis—دیریاز is a poetic adjective meaning 'long-standing/long'.
segments.s496-g2: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'; خورشید is the subject 'the sun' and بنمود is a past tense verb 'appeared/showed itself' used intransitively; چینی طراز is a compound adjectival phrase 'of Chinese fashion/pattern' describing the sun's appearance—طراز functions as 'pattern/style'.
segments.s496-g3: برافراخت is the past tense 'he hoisted/raised' (from بر افراشتن); از کوه زرین 'from the golden mountain' is a from‑phrase giving origin; درفش is the direct object 'banner'—the clause follows a compact, poetic SOV/order with location/origin placed immediately after the verb.
segments.s496-g4: نگونسار شد is a predicative sequence: نگونسار (an adjective 'mournful/ill‑fated') + شد ('became') so 'it became mournful'; پرنیانی بنفش is the predicate complement ('silken purple'), with پرنیانی qualifying the material and بنفش the color—poetic inversion places adjectives after the copula.
segments.s496-g5: سکندر is the subject 'Alexander'; بیامد is the simple past 'came/approached'; به نزدیک شاه is a prepositional phrase 'near the king' or 'into the king's presence'—preposition + adjective + noun express motion toward proximity.
segments.s496-g6: پرستنده is a nominal agent 'the worshipper/devotee' serving as the subject; برخاست is past tense 'rose' or 'stood up/left his place'; از بارگاه ('from the bar̄gāh') marks origin or place of rising—بارگاه here is the royal court/palace.
segments.s496-g7: به رسمی ('in the custom/manner') is a circumstantial phrase ('according to the ritual/custom'); که بودش is a relative clause 'which was' (که = 'that' and بودش = 'it was' enclitic), so به رسمی که بودش = 'in the manner that was/customarily used'; فرود آورید is a past tense plural/formal past 'they brought down/made descend' (آوردید used here to mean 'brought').
segments.s496-g8: جهانجوی is a substantive epithet 'world‑seeker' used as a noun for the ambitious man (here, Alexander); پیش سپهبد is a locative/prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before the spahbed (general)'; چمید is a past verb meaning here roughly 'took his place/sat down'—the exact lexical senses given in the lemma list do not match this poetic verb use, so sense is left unspecified.
segments.s496-g49: ازان سان is an idiom meaning 'in that manner' (ازان = 'from/that', سان = 'like/as'). شوم is the 1st‑person present/subjunctive of شدن used here to state the speaker's intention or prospective state ('I shall be / I will appear'). پیش او = 'before him / in his presence', and با سپاه = 'with an army' (با marks accompaniment); together they modify شوم.
segments.s496-g50: که introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that' / 'that'). بخشایش آرد is a verb phrase where آرد is 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive of آوردن ('may bring/grant') and بخشایش is its object ('forgiveness' or 'mercy'). برو here is a poetic/contractional element (see note) placed before the agents چرخ و ماه; چرخ و ماه ('heaven/sky and moon') are the entities expected to bring that forgiveness.
segments.s496-g51: ازین آزمایش = 'this trial' (از + این = 'from/this'). ندارد زیان is 'has no harm' — ندارد is the present negative of داشتن ('does not have'), and زیان is the object 'harm / loss'. The clause states that the trial causes no damage.
segments.s496-g52: بماند is a stative/optative 3rd‑person form of ماندن meaning 'remains / will remain'. مگر here functions as 'except' (introducing what will remain). دوستی در میان = 'friendship between (them)' where در میان is the prepositional phrase 'between / among'. Together: 'what remains is only friendship between them.'
segments.s496-g53: چه گویید = interrogative 'what will you say?' — چه is 'what' and گویید is a present/subjunctive or deliberative form 'you say / you will say' (poetic usage). و links the clauses. این را marks 'this' as the object (را = definite/object marker) and چه پاسخ دهید = 'what answer will you give?' with پاسخ 'answer' and دهید (دادن) 'give' in 2nd‑person plural/subjunctive or future sense.
segments.s496-g54: مرا (here orthographically split in the text) is the accusative 'me' (م + را). اندرین رای فرخ = 'in this counsel/opinion that is felicitous' (اندرین = 'in this', رای = 'counsel/opinion', فرخ = 'fortunate/auspicious'). نهید (from نهادن/نهاد) is an optative/imperative 2nd‑person plural form 'place / set' — i.e. 'place me in this counsel.'
segments.s496-g55: همه مهتران = 'all the elders/chiefs' (همه = 'all', مهتران = plural 'elders/chiefs'). سر برافراختند is a past tense clause: سر ('head') followed by the compound verb برافراختند (past, 3rd‑person plural of برافراشتن) meaning 'they raised / lifted' — common poetic order places the object (سر) before the verb.
segments.s496-g56: همی is an adverbial particle meaning roughly 'then/so/thereupon' (used as a connective). پاسخ پادشا = 'an answer (for/to the king)' with پاسخ as the object and پادشا 'king' as the beneficiary/indirect object; ساختند is past 3rd‑person plural of ساختن 'made / composed / delivered' — 'they then gave/delivered an answer to the king.'
segments.s496-g73: This clause contains an omitted third‑person subject (he) and two actions; Persian places the verb after its object(s) in classical/poetic order. در گنج literally reads “the door/entrance of the treasure” and here functions idiomatically: «در گنج بگشاد» = “opened the treasury (the door of the vault).” The conjunction و then links the following noun phrase (تاج پدر) as a second item in the sequence of what was dealt with.
segments.s496-g74: This continues the actions: بیاورد is a past/perfective action ('brought') with an implicit subject carried over from the previous clause. با + noun marks accompaniment ('with an attendant'), and the coordinated طوق زر is a compound noun phrase 'a necklace of gold' raised by و. Word order is object(s) before the verb in the verse-level syntax (the verb already appeared earlier for the first action, and the narrative ties the items together).
segments.s496-g75: یکی here introduces 'a certain' (indefinite) item. The noun phrase تاج بد = 'an ill-made/bad crown'; کاندران is an archaic/poetic descriptor here (rendered as 'obscure' in this edition) that qualifies the crown’s condition or provenance, and شهر و مرز ('the city and the frontier') gives the scope/place where it was found or known. Word order places adjectives and qualifiers after the head noun in poetic diction.
segments.s496-g76: This is a single verb clause with an indefinite/negative sense: کسی ... ندانست = 'no one knew.' گوهرش را is the definite object 'its jewel' (را marks the definite object). ندانست is the past negative sense 'did not know' (classical past of دانستن). ارز (at the end) is the object of knowing: 'the worth/value'.
segments.s496-g77: فرستاده را گفت = 'he told/said to the messenger'; را is the object/indirect-object marker. The following clause is quoted or reported content: کین بی‌بهاست — here کین functions as the demonstrative/thing in question (in older usage) and بی‌بهاست is a predicative phrase: 'is without value/worthless.' The verb گفت is the reporting verb introducing this judgment.
segments.s496-g78: This is a generic conditional/relative clause: هرانکس که دارد = 'whoever has/possesses it' (هرانکس = 'whoever', که = relative particle). جزو نارواست is a nominal predicate: جزو 'a part/portion' with ناروا 'unjust/improper' and the copula ست 'is' — together: 'is an unjust/undeserved part/possession.' The verb دارد is present/past depending on context but here marks possession in the general statement.
segments.s496-g79: به تاج مهان = 'for the crown of the nobles/guests' (به marks purpose/assignment). چون سزا دیدمش = 'when/as I saw him worthy' — چون here introduces the reason/timing ('when' or 'as'), سزا is 'desert/worthiness' and دیدمش is the past-tense verb with enclitic object 'I saw him' (دیدم + ش). This whole clause gives the reason for the following choice.
segments.s496-g80: ز فرزند پرمایه = 'from/among the fruitful/able-born children/sons' (ز = poetic 'from/among'). بگزیدمش is a past-tense perfective verb 'I chose him' (ب + گزید + م + ش), with the object enclitic ش 'him'. The verb-final placement is normal and the prepositional z- phrase marks the source group (from among the sons).
segments.s496-g113: همان خود = an emphatic demonstrative + reflexive: 'that very one / he himself'. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and' linking the next noun. مغفر here is a concrete item meaning a covering/veil (likely an armour or cloak in context). The numeral phrase هزار و دویست (one thousand two hundred) follows the noun as a quantity.
segments.s496-g114: به گنجور فرمود = به + noun (toward/into the treasury) + فرمود, used as a verb meaning he ordered/commanded. کاکنون is the fused/poetic form of که اکنون ('that now'), introducing the reported clause. مه‌ایست is a predicative phrase literally 'it is the moon' (here uttered by the speaker as the content of what was said).
segments.s496-g115: همه پاک بر بیطقون برشمار is an imperative instruction: همه 'all' (the totality), پاک here as an adverb = 'completely/cleanly', بر + برشمار (برشمار is an imperative 'count' / 'assign') takes بیطقون as the indirect object or addressee (a name or epithet). Word order puts the object/name after the preposition-like بر and before the verb-form.
segments.s496-g116: بگویش = بگو + ـیش (imperative 'tell him'); که introduces the subordinate clause (that / so that). شبگیر is the noun 'night-visitor' / 'night-guard' (here meaning the night-watch). برساز is an imperative 'make/prepare', and کار ('work/affair') names the task — together: 'Tell him to prepare the night-watch (i.e. get the night-duty ready).'
segments.s496-g117: سپیده چو برزد ز بالا درفش: سپیده 'dawn' is the subject; چو here (= 'when') introduces a temporal clause. برزد is an archaic past verb meaning 'rose / came forth'. ز بالا = 'from above / on high', and درفش 'standard/banner' completes the scene: 'When dawn rose from on high the banner...' (poetic word order places verb early).
segments.s496-g118: چو کافور شد روی چرخ بنفش: چو here continues as a temporal/comparative 'when/as'; کافور 'camphor' supplies a simile ('like camphor'); شد is the past 'became'; روی چرخ = 'the face/surface of the sky' (روی = face, چرخ = sky); بنفش is the resulting adjective 'purple'. Structure: 'and when, like camphor, the face of the sky became purple.'
segments.s496-g119: زمین تازه شد کوه چون سندروس: زمین تازه شد is a simple clause 'the earth became fresh/renewed' (تازه شد = became new). The second clause uses a simile: کوه چون سندروس = 'the mountain like sandalwood' (سندروس = fragrant wood), so the mountain is compared to fragrant/sandalwood — compact poetic parallelism is typical.
segments.s496-g120: ز درگاه برخاست آوای کوس: ز درگاه = 'from the court/threshold' (ز = from; درگاه here = royal court/entrance). برخاست is 'rose / arose' (intransitive past), آوای = 'the sound/voice of', and کوس is 'the war-drum' — together: 'from the court rose the sound of the drum.'
segments.s496-g25: برادر بود is noun + past copula: literally 'was a brother.' نیک‌خواهت is a compound noun 'nīk-khāh' with the enclitic -ت marking 'your' (i.e. 'your well-wisher'). مرا (here written split as م + را) is the beneficiary/object 'to me' — the whole phrase reads, in idiomatic English, 'your well-wisher was to me a brother.'
segments.s496-g26: به جای + noun is a prepositional phrase 'in place of / instead of.' صلیب is the noun 'cross.' است is the copula 'is' (here used in poetic inverted order), گاهت is گاه + ـت 'your time/occasion/presence,' and مرا again marks 'to me/for me.' The clause thus means 'your presence is, for me, in place of a cross.'
segments.s496-g27: نگه کرد is a light-verb construction: نگه (a look / gaze) + کرد (past of 'to do') = 'he looked' / 'he fixed his gaze.' قیدافه is a proper name; سوگند is the noun 'oath.' اوی is the archaic third-person clitic 'he/his' tied to سوگند here (i.e. 'Qeydāfeh swore' / 'his oath').
segments.s496-g28: یگانه is an adjective 'single/only' modifying دل 'heart.' و links the two coordinate items; راست is an adjective 'right/true' modifying پیوند 'bond/connection.' اوی again is the third-person clitic 'his' — the phrase states in compact, poetic form that 'his heart was one and his bond true.'
segments.s496-g29: همه کاخ functions adverbially as 'throughout the palace' or 'in the whole palace.' کرسی زرین is a noun phrase 'golden seat/throne' (زرین = 'golden' after the noun in poetic word order). نهاد is the past of نهادن 'to place/put' — here 'he placed/installed.'
segments.s496-g30: به پیش is a prepositional phrase 'before / in front of.' اندر = 'in/within.' آرایش = 'arrangement/ornamentation' and چین = 'fold(s)/array' (here describing drapery or decorative arrangement). نهاد repeats the verb 'placed.' The line means he set it before (them) amid the decorative folds/arrangement.
segments.s496-g31: بخواند is a past tense verb here meaning 'he called/summoned' (one of the common meanings of خواندن). بزرگان و نیک‌اختران را has را marking the definite direct object: 'the elders and the fortunate/auspicious ones.' The verb carries the action 'he summoned them.'
segments.s496-g32: یکایک is an adverb 'one by one' / 'each in turn.' بر آن کرسی زر = 'upon that golden seat/throne' (بر = on/upon; آن = that; کرسی زر = golden throne). نشاند is an archaic/past causative form meaning 'he seated (them)' (the classical past of نشاندن/نشستن used in epic). The object had been marked earlier by را, so the verb appears here without repeating it.
segments.s496-g65: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. اسکندری is the proper name 'Alexander/Iskandar' in the nominative. کو is an archaic/poetic particle here functioning like که ('who/that') attached to the verb, introducing the clause 'who/that comes'; بیاید is a present/subjunctive/future‑oriented 3rd‑person singular 'comes'/'may come'. ز + روم is a prepositional phrase 'from Rum/Byzantium' (ز is the poetic form of از). The whole segment = 'When Alexander, who comes from Rum...'.
segments.s496-g66: به شمشیر is an instrumental phrase 'by/with (the) sword' (به used instrumentally here). دریا is the direct object 'the sea'. کند is the 3rd‑person singular present/optative of کردن 'to make/do' used causatively: 'makes (something) (do something)'. روی بوم is a compound where روی 'face' or 'aspect' takes بوم 'homeland/native soil' as its reference: 'turn the sea's face toward the land.' Word order is verb-final in the clause '... (he) makes the sea turn its face toward the homeland.'
segments.s496-g67: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle meaning 'always/still/continually' marking habitual or general action. از درت is a prepositional phrase 'from your dar(t)' (درت here is a possessive form, literally 'your dar/pearl/treasure' in the line). بازگردد is 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive of بازگشتن 'to return' (used to state a general/resultative action). به چیز literally 'to a thing' (i.e. to mere stuff). Together the clause says that things issuing from your court/treasure will be reduced back to mere things.
segments.s496-g68: همه چیز دنیی is the noun phrase 'all worldly things' (همه چیز = everything; دنیی qualifies it as 'of the world/temporal'). نیرزد is a negative stative verb meaning 'is not worth' (3rd‑person singular). پشیز is the measure/noun 'a trifle/scrap' serving as the standard of worth. Word order presents the subject phrase followed by the predicate 'is not worth a trifle.'
segments.s496-g69: جز از introduces an exception 'except by/through'. آشتی ما is a genitive construction 'our reconciliation' (آشتی = reconciliation, ما = our/we). نبینیم is 1st‑person plural present/optative/negative form 'we do not see' or 'we shall not see' (here expressing that one will see no face except by reconciliation). روی is 'face' (object of نبینیم). The clause means 'we will not see (your) face except through our reconciliation.'
segments.s496-g70: نه negates the following predicate. والا بود is the past/copular construction 'was not exalted/noble' (والا = exalted/noble; بود = was). مردم کینه‌جوی is the nominal predicate 'the people (were) revenge‑seeking' with کینه‌جوی as an adjectival/nominal attribute. The line states in effect that the people were not noble but vengeful.
segments.s496-g71: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is the simple past 'heard' (3rd‑person). گفتار آن is the noun phrase 'that speech' (گفتار = speech, آن = that). بخردان is a dative/agentive plural 'the wise' or 'the wise ones' (here indicating who heard). The clause sets the time: 'When the wise heard that speech...' (subject/agent is expressed by بخردان).
segments.s496-g72: پسندیده is a verbal adjective/participle meaning 'found pleasing' or 'pleasing/approved' (here functioning as predicate: 'it pleased'). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. پاک‌دل modifies موبدان: 'pure‑hearted mobeds' (پاک‌دل = pure‑hearted; موبدان = mobeds, Zoroastrian priests). Together the phrase indicates that the speech was pleasing to the pure‑hearted priests, or that the priests were pleased and were pure‑hearted.
segments.s496-g17: 'به' here is the preposition used as an oath/adjunct meaning 'by'; the three nouns are coordinated with و in a list (زنار = 'girdle', شماس = 'sacristan' or church assistant, روح‌القدس = 'the Holy Spirit'). The whole phrase functions as an oath-like invocation introducing the following statement.
segments.s496-g18: کزین پس = 'henceforth / from this on'. 'م' is an archaic, highly contracted copular/auxiliary (poetic form of 'باشد' / 'is/will be') used here before the noun phrase; 'را' is the definite/object marker attached in the clause; 'خاک' (literally 'earth' or 'dust') is the thing whose location is being stated, and 'در اندلس' gives the place 'in al‑Andalus'. The verb/copula is elliptical/poetic, so the order is compressed.
segments.s496-g19: This clause contains an implied purpose/result nuance introduced by the verb نبیند ('may it not be seen' / 'so that it is not seen') followed by a first‑person assertion. 'نه' negates the speaker's action; 'لشکر' is the object 'army' and 'فرستم' is the first‑person present/subjunctive/optative 'I send' used here in the sense 'I will send' (poetic/future sense). 'به جنگ' means 'to war'. The line reads as a negative statement about sending troops.
segments.s496-g20: نیامیزم is first‑person present negative 'I do not mix / I will not mingle'. 'از هر دری' literally 'from every Dari' — here 'Dari' = a form of Persian or, poetically, 'every tongue/register' — and thus 'از هر دری نیز رنگ' means 'nor will I borrow/assume any colour/appearance from any (other) tongue'. 'نیز' links this negative clause to the preceding negative; 'رنگ' here means 'complexion/appearance'.
segments.s496-g21: The clause is negated by the initial نه. 'با' = 'with' (marking relation/interaction), 'پاک' modifies 'فرزند' = 'pure/innocent son', 'تو' is the 2nd‑person possessive pronoun placed after the noun (poetic placement), and 'بد کنم' = 'I do harm / I treat badly' where کنَم is the first‑person present/subjunctive used as future/intent. Together: 'I will not do ill to your pure son.'
segments.s496-g22: This continues the series of negatives: 'نه فرمان دهم نیز' = 'nor will I give commands either' ('فرمان' = 'command', 'دهم' = 'I give'). The following 'و نه خود کنم' = 'and nor will I do it myself' — 'خود' emphasises 'myself personally' and 'کنم' is again first‑person 'I do'. The paired نه ... نه is a standard correlative negation in Persian.
segments.s496-g23: به جان is an oath formula 'by my life'. یاد دارم is a light‑verb construction meaning 'I keep in memory / I remember' (یاد = 'remembrance', دارم = 'I hold'). 'وفای ترا' = 'your fidelity/loyalty' with the 2nd‑person suffix ت and the object/definiteness marker را; the whole clause is an emphatic assertion of remembering the addressee's faithfulness.
segments.s496-g24: نجویم is the first‑person negative subjunctive/optative 'I will not seek' or 'I shall not desire'. 'به چیزی' = 'for anything / for any reason' and 'جفای ترا' = 'to do you injustice / your wrong' (جفا = cruelty/injustice, with the 2nd‑person suffix and را marking it). Together: 'I will not, for anything, seek to wrong you.'
segments.s496-g9: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from'; word order is prepositional phrase + noun (ز + بیگانه ایوان). ایوان is the place from which the action takes place; بپرداختند is a past, 3rd-person-plural perfective verb (پرداختن) used here in a dynamic sense ('set about / sprang / moved off') and links this motion to the following clause.
segments.s496-g10: فرستاده را is a definite direct object (را marks the object). پیش او is a locative/adverbial phrase 'in the presence of / before him'; the verb تاختند is past, 3rd-person-plural ('they rushed/galloped/ran') and completes the coordinated action begun in the previous segment (they sprang from the portico and then rushed the envoy forward).
segments.s496-g11: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; قیدافه را is the definite object (را) of دید ('he saw'). بر تخت is a prepositional phrase 'upon the throne' and گفت is the past 3rd-person-singular 'he said' (classical narrative order places the verb after its complements).
segments.s496-g12: که introduces reported speech or a content clause ('that / that it may be so'); با رای تو is an instrumental/agent phrase 'by your counsel' (با + رای + تو). مشتری باد is an optative formula: مشتری 'Jupiter' + باد (here the optative particle 'may') expressing a wish, and جفت is the predicate 'paired/matched' — together: 'may Jupiter be paired.'
segments.s496-g13: بدین is a compound/prepositional phrase meaning 'by/with this' or 'thus'; مسیحا is a vocative 'O Messiah' (vocative -ا ending). به فرمان راست is another prepositional phrase 'by the right/righteous command/decree' (به + فرمان + راست), functioning as an adverbial modifier specifying the authority of what follows.
segments.s496-g14: بد ارنده is a nominal phrase 'the ill/evil-bearer' (بد + ارنده), and کو here introduces a relative clause ('who' / 'that'); بر زبانم is a locative/figurative phrase 'upon my tongue' meaning 'in my speech' or 'testifying against me'; گواست is an archaic/poetic predicate related to 'گواهم' / 'گواست' meaning 'is witness' or 'testifies'. Grammatically this whole segment is a relative clause describing the 'ill-bearer' of the previous segment.
segments.s496-g15: با introduces a list of instruments or causes. برای here functions roughly as 'for' or 'on behalf of'; و links items (و دین و صلیب بزرگ). صلیب بزرگ is a noun phrase 'the great cross' (صلیب + بزرگ) — the entire segment is a coordinated noun phrase introduced adverbially by با/برای.
segments.s496-g16: به introduces the purpose/beneficiary ('for'); جان و سر is a coordinated pair 'life and head' (both used idiomatically for 'being' and 'person/leadership'); شهریار is the noun 'king' and سترگ is an epithet 'mighty / great' used postpositively. This segment gives the object or beneficiary for which the gifts/acts of the previous segment are intended.
segments.s496-g153: بلرزید is a past-tense verb (3rd person) meaning 'he/it trembled' — the verb comes first in this poetic word order. طینوش is a proper name (the subject). بر جای خویش is a prepositional phrase 'on/in his place'; خوی + the clitic ش together mark possession ('his own').
segments.s496-g154: پشیمان شد is a copular/periphrastic past: the adjective پشیمان 'regretful' with شد 'became' → 'he became regretful'. از marks the domain/thing he regrets ('about' or 'concerning'). دانش و رای خویش = 'his knowledge and his counsel/opinion' with خوی + ش again forming the possessive 'his'.
segments.s496-g155: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت here functions as the verb 'said' in narrative past). کای is a vocative particle 'O', followed by شاه 'king' and the epithet برترمنش 'high-minded/proud' postposed to the noun.
segments.s496-g156: ستایش گزینی is an exhortative/optative sense: گزینی (second-person present/optative) 'choose' with ستایش 'praise' as its object. به از ... is the comparative idiom 'better than / rather than' (به از سرزنش = 'better than reproach').
segments.s496-g157: چنان هم که is a comparative/connecting phrase 'just so / as'. با خویش من = 'with myself' (خوی + ش + من renders the reflexive/possessive sense 'my own/self'); قیدروش (literally 'fetter/way') here means a restraint or the constraint of one's habit/way — this clause sets up the comparison for the imperative that follows.
segments.s496-g158: بزرگی کن is an imperative 'do greatness / be great' (بزرگی noun + کن imperative 'do'). و links the two imperatives. راستی را بکوش = 'strive for truth' where را marks راستی as a definite object and بکوش is the second-person imperative/jussive 'strive'.
segments.s496-g159: نه introduces a rhetorical negative question. این بود پیمانت = 'was this your pact' where پیمانت contains the 2nd-person suffix -ت ('your pact'). با مادرم = 'with my mother' (با = 'with', مادر + م = possessive 'my mother'). The clause questions the addressee about a prior promise.
segments.s496-g160: نگفتی is past-second-person 'didn't you say' (here used to introduce reported speech). که introduces the content clause. از marks separation 'from' in از راستی نگذرم = 'that I will not depart/leave the truth'. نگذرم is a negative present/subjunctive/jussive 'I will not pass/abandon', used as a pledged stance.
segments.s496-g97: پلنگی is the noun ‘leopard’ used adjectivally/nominally (here: the leopard‑skin). که introduces a relative clause modifying پلنگی. خوانی is used as a verb meaning 'call' (classical usage: 'they call'), with همی before the verb as the archaic/prosodic progressive/habitual marker (‘they habitually/then call’). بربری is the name/epithet being predicated by خوانی ('called “Borbari”').
segments.s496-g98: ازان = 'from that / of that' (referring back to the leopard‑skin). چار صد = the numeral phrase 'four hundred' modifying پوست. پوست = 'skins/hides'. بد here is an archaic form (contracted) of بود ('was') used as the past auxiliary; it does not mean 'bad' in this line. بر سری = a prepositional phrase 'upon the head/top' (i.e. laid on top of it). Word order puts the numeral before the noun and the past copula before the locative phrase.
segments.s496-g99: ز (poetic form of از) marks the material/source 'from'. چرم گوزن is the nominal compound 'deer leather / deerskin' (noun + noun). ملمع is an adjective meaning 'polished' (classical/poetic adjective) modifying the skins; the numeral (هزار) quantifies them (a thousand). The sequence is typical classical Persian: preposition + noun phrase + adjective + numeral.
segments.s496-g100: همه رنگ و بیرنگ = 'both coloured and uncoloured' (همه here 'all / both', and و coordinates the two opposing adjectives). او is the pronoun referring back to the object being described (the covering/decoration). پر as an adjective/particle here means 'full (of)' and takes نگار 'ornament/decoration' as its complement: together 'full of ornament' (i.e. richly decorated).
segments.s496-g101: دگر صد = 'another hundred' (دگر = 'other/another' + numeral). سگ و یوز = 'dogs and cheetahs' linked by و. نخچیر گیر is a postpositive descriptive compound: نخچیر 'prey/game' + گیر 'catcher' (i.e. 'game‑catchers' or 'those that seize prey'); گیر functions as an agentive/qualifying element modifying the animals.
segments.s496-g102: که introduces a relative clause describing the animals. آهو is the object 'the gazelle'; ورا is the enclitic object pronoun 'it / to it' referring to the gazelle. پیش here has the sense 'before / in front (previously)' and, together with دیدی (an archaic past form 'saw' / 'had seen'), gives 'who had seen the gazelle beforehand'. ز تیر 'by/by means of an arrow' indicates the arrow as the relevant circumstance/agent; the precise scopal relation is somewhat ambiguous in poetry (it can mean 'seen the gazelle before the arrow [struck]' or 'seen the gazelle that was struck by an arrow') but grammatically z+noun marks the instrument/source.
segments.s496-g103: بیاورد is the past tense verb 'he brought' (3rd singular) used narratively. زان پس = 'after that' (زان = 'from that / of that' + پس 'after/then'). دوصد = 'two hundred' quantifies گاومیش 'water‑buffalo' (noun). The verb governs the whole noun phrase as its object.
segments.s496-g104: پرستنده او = 'his attendants / those devoted to him' (پرستنده used as 'followers/attendants'). همی before the verb randed (راند) is the archaic progressive/habitual marker; راند is the past stem 'drove / caused to move'. پیش is an adverb 'in front / before', so the clause means 'his attendants drove them forward before him.' Word order: subject (پرستنده او) + auxiliary همی + verb راند + adverbial پیش.

sentences.s496-s97: He said to Qeydafeh, "Speak, O alert woman, world-holder and bīnâdel and counsellor."
sentences.s496-s98: I will keep my fidelity to you while I live; I have filled the soul with your love.
sentences.s496-s73: The king then chose from the army a thousand of those famed Romans.
sentences.s496-s74: The armored men with the Gavaruy mace advanced — a quarrelsome troop.
sentences.s496-s75: All the men drew up around that thicket, forming a battle line with weapons.
sentences.s496-s76: Sekandar cried out, 'O swift man! If the thought of war comes to you, flee.'
sentences.s496-s21: He continually seeks to make us suffer for the sake of treasure; all the treasure of the world is not worth that suffering.
sentences.s496-s22: I am resolved that we will not make war with him; nor will I bring hardship upon the kingdom.
sentences.s496-s23: Let us give him a prudent answer; let us raise his head and give him counsel.
sentences.s496-s24: If he seeks war after my counsel, then — after my counsel — let him behold my bonds.
sentences.s496-s85: The sovereign took his hand in his hand, in that manner with which he had said he bound the covenant.
sentences.s496-s86: To him he said, 'Choose gentleness and tranquility; I hold no grudge in my heart against you.'
sentences.s496-s87: When your mother sat on the golden throne, I placed my person into your hand just so.
sentences.s496-s88: I said that I would likewise place the kingly power of the land into your hand.
sentences.s496-s45: Each stone weighed two mithqals; its color was like a single ember of fire.
sentences.s496-s46: The emerald was in four hundred pieces; in its greenness it was like the arc of a rainbow and did not fade.
